title: College Prep Pressure
description: Junior year. The college counselor says your child needs more extracurriculars, better test scores, and a 'compelling narrative' for applications. Your child is already stretched thin.

choices.text: Invest in SAT prep courses and resume-building activities
choices.result_text: Your child's application looks impressive on paper. But the pressure is immense and they're running on caffeine and anxiety.

choices.text: Reassure them — there are many paths to success beyond elite colleges
choices.result_text: Your child relaxes slightly. They focus on what genuinely interests them rather than what looks good. The applications are authentic if not flashy.

choices.text: Explore trade school and alternative paths — college isn't for everyone
choices.result_text: Your child is relieved to hear that college isn't mandatory. They start researching skilled trades and apprenticeships with genuine excitement.

title: First Job
description: Your 16-year-old wants to get a part-time job at the local fast food restaurant. It would mean less study time but they'd have their own money. They're tired of asking you for everything.

choices.text: Support it — real-world experience and financial independence are valuable
choices.result_text: Your child learns about taxes, time management, and dealing with difficult customers. They appreciate money more and waste less.

choices.text: Only if they maintain their grades — school comes first
choices.result_text: The condition works — your child proves they can juggle both. Time management becomes a strength. They're exhausted but proud.

choices.text: Say no — these are the last years to focus purely on education
choices.result_text: Your child resents the dependency. Their friends have money and freedom while they still ask permission for everything.

title: Drunk Driving Call
description: It's 1 AM. Your child calls from a party, clearly scared. Their friend who was supposed to drive is drunk. They need a ride home. At least they called you.

choices.text: Pick them up immediately, no questions tonight — talk tomorrow
choices.result_text: You drive in silence. Tomorrow's conversation is serious but fair. Your child knows they can always call you. That trust might save their life someday.

choices.text: Pick them up but ground them immediately — they shouldn't have been there
choices.result_text: Your child is safe but regrets calling you. Next time they might not. The punishment for honesty sends a complicated message.

choices.text: Tell them to call an Uber — they got themselves into this
choices.result_text: Your child figures it out alone. They're safe but the message is clear — they can't rely on you. They stop calling when they're in trouble.

timeline_text: Late-night safety call from a party

title: Senioritis
description: Second semester senior year. Your child has been accepted to college but their grades are plummeting. They skip class, sleep until noon on weekends, and say 'it doesn't matter anymore.'

choices.text: Warn them — colleges can rescind acceptances for tanking grades
choices.result_text: The reality check works. Your child pulls grades back to acceptable levels. Not their best work, but enough to keep their acceptance.

choices.text: Let them enjoy it — they've earned some relaxation after years of pressure
choices.result_text: Your child enjoys the freedom. Their grades drop but not catastrophically. They enter college rested but with weakened study habits.

choices.text: Use this time for life skills — cooking, budgeting, laundry
choices.result_text: Instead of fighting about grades, you teach practical skills. Your child enters adulthood knowing how to feed themselves and manage money.

title: Caught Drinking
description: You come home early to find your child and friends drinking beer in the basement. They're not wasted but clearly tipsy. Empty cans are everywhere. They freeze when they see you.

choices.text: Send friends home, then have a calm conversation about alcohol
choices.result_text: You discuss alcohol honestly — the risks, the law, the family history. Your child respects the conversation and is more careful going forward.

choices.text: Explode — call all the parents, ground your child indefinitely
choices.result_text: The nuclear response creates chaos. Other parents are angry at you for 'overreacting.' Your child is humiliated and furious. They'll just be more careful next time.

choices.text: Acknowledge experimentation is normal but set clear boundaries
choices.result_text: Your measured response surprises your child. They expected anger. The boundaries are set but the relationship stays intact.

title: The Breakup
description: Your child's first serious relationship just ended badly. They've been in their room for three days, barely eating, missing school. You can hear them crying at night.

choices.text: Give them space but leave the door open — literally and figuratively
choices.result_text: After a few days, your child emerges. They don't want to talk about it yet, but knowing you're there without pressure helps them process.

choices.text: Share your own heartbreak stories — show them they'll survive this
choices.result_text: Your vulnerability surprises them. Knowing you went through this too — and survived — gives them hope. You become more human in their eyes.

choices.text: Tough love — 'You're 16. This isn't the end of the world. Get up.'
choices.result_text: Your child gets up, but something closes off. They learn that vulnerability isn't safe around you. Future heartbreaks will be handled alone.

title: Car Accident
description: Your newly-licensed teen rear-ends someone at a stoplight. No one is hurt but the car is damaged. They're shaking and terrified. The other driver is angry.

choices.text: Handle the situation calmly, then use it as a learning moment
choices.result_text: You model calm crisis management. Your child learns about insurance, responsibility, and that mistakes don't have to be catastrophic.

choices.text: Revoke driving privileges — they're clearly not ready
choices.result_text: Your child loses independence and has to be driven everywhere again. They feel infantilized and the accident becomes a source of shame rather than learning.

choices.text: Make them pay for the deductible from their own earnings
choices.result_text: Working to pay off the deductible teaches real consequences. Your child becomes a much more careful driver and understands the cost of carelessness.

title: College Application Essays
description: Your child is staring at a blank screen. The essay prompt asks them to describe a challenge they've overcome. They say their life is 'too boring' to write about.

choices.text: Help them brainstorm — remind them of challenges they've forgotten
choices.result_text: Together you uncover stories they'd dismissed as ordinary. The essay becomes genuine and compelling. The process brings you closer.

choices.text: Hire a college essay consultant
choices.result_text: The consultant produces a polished essay. It's well-written but your child feels like a fraud. They wonder if they got in on their own merit.

choices.text: Let them struggle with it alone — this is their story to tell
choices.result_text: After days of frustration, your child produces something raw and honest. It's not perfect but it's authentically them.

title: Pregnancy Scare
description: Your teenager comes to you terrified — they think they or their partner might be pregnant. They're shaking and can barely speak. This is every parent's nightmare scenario.

choices.text: Stay calm, get a test, and deal with whatever comes together
choices.result_text: The test is negative. The relief is overwhelming. But the conversation that follows about protection and responsibility is the most important one you've ever had.

choices.text: React with anger and disappointment — 'How could you be so irresponsible?'
choices.result_text: Your child shuts down completely. Whether the scare resolves or not, they'll never come to you with something vulnerable again.

choices.text: Take them to a clinic for proper testing and counseling
choices.result_text: Professional guidance helps both of you. The clinic provides education and resources. Your child makes more informed choices going forward.

title: Dropping Out Talk
description: Your child sits you down and says they want to drop out of high school. They have a plan — sort of. They want to start a business, or get their GED later, or 'figure it out.'

choices.text: Listen to their reasoning, then present the statistics calmly
choices.result_text: The data about lifetime earnings and opportunities gives them pause. They agree to finish but with the understanding that college isn't mandatory after.

choices.text: Absolutely not — you're finishing high school, end of discussion
choices.result_text: Your child complies resentfully. They finish high school but the authoritarian approach damages your relationship during a critical time.

choices.text: Challenge them — 'Show me a real business plan and we'll talk'
choices.result_text: To your surprise, they actually write a business plan. It's naive but shows real thinking. You negotiate: finish school while building the business on the side.

title: Prom Night
description: Prom is approaching. Your child wants to rent a limo with friends, buy an expensive outfit, and attend an after-party. The total cost would be over $500. They say 'everyone is doing this.'

choices.text: Split the cost — you'll cover half if they earn the rest
choices.result_text: Your child works extra shifts and contributes their share. They appreciate the night more because they earned it. The photos are worth every penny.

choices.text: Cover it all — this is a once-in-a-lifetime memory
choices.result_text: Your child has a magical night. The memories and photos last forever. You're glad you could give them this, even if it stretched the budget.

choices.text: Set a reasonable budget — they can have fun without excess
choices.result_text: Your child gets creative — thrift store outfit, carpooling instead of limo. They have fun and learn that experiences don't require extravagance.

title: Gap Year Request
description: Instead of going straight to college, your child wants to take a gap year. They want to travel, work, and 'find themselves.' You worry they'll never go back to school.

choices.text: Support it with structure — they must have a plan and savings goals
choices.result_text: Your child creates a structured gap year plan. They work, save, and gain life experience. They enter college a year later with maturity and purpose.

choices.text: Insist on college immediately — momentum matters
choices.result_text: Your child goes to college but isn't fully committed. They struggle with motivation freshman year, wondering what they missed.

choices.text: Compromise — defer admission for one semester, not a full year
choices.result_text: The compromise satisfies both of you. Your child gets a taste of independence and enters college in January refreshed and motivated.

title: Caught with Marijuana
description: The school calls — your child was found with marijuana on campus. It's an automatic suspension and possible expulsion. Your child says it was for anxiety and 'everyone uses it.'

choices.text: Accept school consequences and address the underlying anxiety
choices.result_text: The suspension is embarrassing but the therapy for anxiety is life-changing. Your child finds healthier coping mechanisms.

choices.text: Fight the school's zero-tolerance policy — it's too harsh
choices.result_text: You reduce the punishment but damage the relationship with the school. Your child learns that rules can be argued away with a persistent parent.

choices.text: Severe consequences at home on top of school punishment
choices.result_text: The double punishment feels excessive to your child. They don't use at school again but the underlying anxiety remains unaddressed.

title: Leadership Opportunity
description: Your child is nominated for student body president. They're excited but terrified of public speaking and the campaign process. They ask if you think they should run.

choices.text: Enthusiastically support them — help with speech practice and campaign
choices.result_text: Win or lose, the campaign transforms your child. They discover they can inspire people. Public speaking becomes a strength rather than a fear.

choices.text: Be realistic — 'It's a lot of work. Are you sure you can handle it with everything else?'
choices.result_text: Your caution plants doubt. Your child decides not to run and watches someone less qualified win. They wonder 'what if' for years.

choices.text: Let them decide completely on their own — don't influence either way
choices.result_text: Your child makes the decision independently. Whatever they choose, they own it fully. The autonomy itself is empowering.

title: Graduation Day
description: It's here. Eighteen years of raising this child, and they're walking across a stage in cap and gown. As you watch them receive their diploma, you reflect on the journey.

choices.text: Tell them how proud you are — every struggle was worth it
choices.result_text: Your words mean everything. In this moment, all the fights, the worry, the sleepless nights — it all led here. Your child hugs you and whispers 'thank you.'

choices.text: Give them a meaningful gift that represents their journey
choices.result_text: The gift becomes a treasured possession — a symbol of everything you've been through together. They carry it into their new life.

choices.text: Remind them this is just the beginning — the real challenges are ahead
choices.result_text: Even on graduation day, you can't just celebrate. Your child nods but wishes you'd just said 'I'm proud of you' without the caveat.

title: Friend in Crisis
description: Your child's close friend is in serious trouble — possibly suicidal, definitely self-harming. Your child is carrying this secret alone and it's crushing them. They finally tell you.

choices.text: Contact the friend's parents immediately — this is too serious for kids to handle alone
choices.result_text: The friend's parents are shocked but grateful. Professional help is arranged. Your child feels guilty for 'betraying' the secret but relieved the burden is shared.

choices.text: Help your child support their friend while you quietly alert the school counselor
choices.result_text: The counselor intervenes discreetly. Your child maintains the friendship while professionals handle the crisis. A delicate balance.

choices.text: Tell your child it's not their responsibility — they can't save everyone
choices.result_text: Your child pulls back from the friendship. The friend's situation may or may not resolve. Your child feels guilty but also lighter.
